blurb: Indexing, sharding, replication, consistency, SQL vs NoSQL.

questions.q: A query needs to return rows ordered by (created_at DESC). Which index is best?
choices: B-tree on created_at with DESC order
No index — ORDER BY is always fast
questions.explanation: B-tree supports range queries AND sorted iteration — critical for ORDER BY. Hash indexes only support equality lookups. For descending, some engines need an explicit DESC index.

questions.q: You're choosing between hash sharding and range sharding for a time-series workload. Which creates more hotspots?
choices: Neither — time series needs no sharding
questions.explanation: Range sharding by timestamp puts ALL recent writes on the latest shard — classic hotspot. Hash sharding spreads writes, but loses range-query locality. The tradeoff is always in play.

questions.q: In a Postgres write-ahead-log (WAL), why does group commit improve throughput?
choices: Batches many commits into one fsync, amortizing the disk-flush cost
questions.explanation: Each fsync costs 1–10ms. Committing 100 transactions in one fsync beats 100 individual fsyncs. Trade: durability window is bounded by commit interval, typically 5–10ms.

choices: A latency-vs-consistency tradeoff when no partition exists
questions.explanation: PACELC: during a Partition, choose Availability or Consistency; Else, choose Latency or Consistency. Dynamo is PA/EL (always latency). Spanner is PC/EC (always consistency).

questions.q: Quorum read/write with N=3 replicas. If R=2 and W=2, is consistency guaranteed?
questions.explanation: R+W > N guarantees overlap between readers and latest writer. N=3, R=2, W=2 → overlap. Dynamo's default is exactly this.

questions.q: LSM trees vs B-trees: which is generally faster for write-heavy workloads?
questions.explanation: LSM: writes go to an in-memory buffer + sequential WAL, then flushed as sorted SSTables. No random-write amplification. Read amp is higher (check multiple SSTables). RocksDB/Cassandra/LevelDB are LSM.

questions.q: A multi-tenant SaaS database uses one schema-per-tenant vs one row-per-tenant. What's the main operational downside of schema-per-tenant?
choices: Schema migrations must apply to every tenant schema
questions.explanation: Schema-per-tenant: 10K tenants = 10K migrations per deploy. Shared-schema with tenant_id is simpler to migrate but weaker isolation. Mid-tier: schema groups or DB-per-tenant for large customers.

questions.q: Which type of replication has the WORST RPO (potential data loss window) in exchange for the lowest write latency?
questions.explanation: Async replication: writes ack immediately; replication lag can be seconds. If primary crashes, unreplicated writes are lost. Sync replication ensures zero loss but adds RTT to every write.
